planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; seeds are tiny and require light to germinate — surface-sow and do not cover. Transplant outdoors after all frost danger has passed into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. In zones 9-11, stevia is a true perennial; in zones 7-8, treat as a tender annual or overwinter cuttings indoors.

directions: Stevia rebaudiana is a tender perennial herb native to the subtropical highland borderlands of Paraguay and Brazil, where indigenous Guarani peoples have used its intensely sweet leaves for centuries as a sweetener and medicinal plant; the steviol glycosides in its leaves are 30-300 times sweeter than sucrose, making even a few fresh leaves a potent, calorie-free sweetening agent for teas, lemonades, and desserts. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost date by surface-sowing onto moist, sterile seed-starting mix — seeds need light to germinate and must not be covered; germination is often erratic, and cuttings from established plants root readily and produce more uniform plants than seed-grown specimens. Transplant hardened seedlings into well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; stevia dislikes waterlogged conditions but benefits from consistent moisture and regular feeding during the growing season. Harvest leaves throughout summer by pinching branch tips to encourage bushiness; dry harvested leaves completely before crumbling them for storage as a concentrated sweetener, or use fresh leaves directly in beverages.

planting.note: Propagate from semi-hardwood cuttings taken in late spring or early summer; root cuttings in moist, well-drained medium and transplant to the garden after last frost in full sun and well-drained soil. In zone 9 plants may die back to the roots in a hard frost but regrow vigorously in spring; in zones 10-11 Mexican oregano grows as an evergreen shrub year-round.

directions: Lippia graveolens is the true oregano of Mexican and Tex-Mex cooking — a shrubby, woody perennial native to dry, rocky hillsides from northern Mexico through Central America whose strongly aromatic, slightly rough-textured leaves have a more intense, earthy, and complex flavor than Mediterranean oregano (Origanum vulgare), making it the preferred herb in authentic salsa, mole, chili, and carne asada recipes. Propagate from semi-hardwood stem cuttings, which root readily in moist perlite or sand; Mexican oregano is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in full sun, hot conditions, and lean, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil — rich, moist soils reduce essential oil concentration and can cause root rot. In zones 9-11 it grows as a multi-stemmed shrub 2-4 feet tall, bearing small, white, tubular flowers attractive to bees and butterflies throughout the warm season; in zone 9 a hard freeze may kill top growth, but established plants typically regrow vigorously from the roots in spring. Harvest leafy branch tips at any time during the growing season; the flavor is most concentrated just before flowering and the leaves dry well, intensifying in aroma much like Mediterranean oregano.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in early spring around the last frost date in well-drained, fertile, moist soil in full sun; thin seedlings to 6-8 inches apart within clumps. Garlic chives self-seed prolifically — deadhead spent flower heads promptly to prevent unwanted spread throughout the garden.

directions: Allium tuberosum, commonly known as garlic chives or Chinese chives, is a vigorous, clump-forming perennial allium native to eastern Asia and widely cultivated throughout China, Japan, and Southeast Asia as both a culinary herb and a vegetable; unlike common chives, its flat, strap-like leaves carry a mild but distinct garlic flavor, and its star-shaped white flowers borne on upright scapes in late summer are both ornamental and edible. Direct-sow seeds in early spring in moderately fertile, consistently moist but well-drained soil in full sun; plants are more drought-tolerant than common chives once established but produce the most flavorful and abundant foliage with consistent moisture. Harvest leaves by cutting to within 1-2 inches of the ground, repeating every few weeks throughout the season; garlic chives are also commonly grown in the dark to produce blanched, yellow-etiolated leaves (jiu huang) prized in Chinese cooking for their tenderer texture and milder flavor. Deadhead the attractive white flower clusters before seeds ripen, as garlic chives self-sow prolifically and can become invasive in favorable garden conditions; established clumps benefit from division every 3-4 years in early spring to maintain vigor.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center (AVRDC)

planting.note: Direct-sow on or just before the last frost date; seeds benefit from light cold stratification and can be surface-sown or barely covered with fine soil in a sunny, well-drained position. Common vervain self-sows readily once established and naturalizes well in dry, open, disturbed ground.

directions: Verbena officinalis is an ancient medicinal herb with one of the longest recorded histories of use in European and Near Eastern herbal tradition, valued from classical antiquity through the Middle Ages as a nervine tonic, digestive bitter, and sacred ritual plant; it is a slender, upright perennial with deeply lobed, rough-textured gray-green leaves and long, wiry spikes of tiny, pale lilac to white flowers throughout summer that are visited enthusiastically by small native bees and hoverflies. Direct-sow seeds in a dry, sunny, well-drained position — common vervain is a plant of waste ground, roadsides, dry grasslands, and disturbed soils and thrives in lean, free-draining, neutral to alkaline conditions where more vigorous herbs might be out-competed; it will decline in rich, moist, or shaded sites. Harvest the leafy, flowering tops during full bloom for use fresh or dried in traditional herbal preparations; the bitter iridoid glycosides (particularly verbenalin) give the plant its characteristic medicinal properties valued by herbalists for supporting the nervous system and digestion. Common vervain self-sows reliably and naturalizes easily in gravel gardens, dry banks, and cottage herb gardens, where it adds an airy, wild-flower quality while maintaining its important role as a medicinal herb and pollinator plant.
sources: Jekka McVicar's Jekka's Complete Herb Book

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring as soon as growth resumes, or in early fall; replant divisions 8-12 inches apart in well-drained, fertile, moist soil in full sun. Division every 3-4 years maintains plant vigor and prevents clumps from becoming congested.

directions: Allium schoenoprasum var. sibiricum, commonly called Siberian chives or giant chives, is a robust, cold-hardy variety of common chives native to the harsh continental climates of Siberia, northern China, and Alaska, distinguished by its noticeably broader, thicker, hollow leaves and larger, showier lavender-pink flowerheads that make it both a more productive culinary herb and a bolder ornamental plant than standard chives. Propagate by dividing established clumps in early spring or fall, replanting divisions at the same depth in moderately fertile, consistently moist but well-drained soil in full sun; Siberian chives are more tolerant of cold, wind, and dry conditions than common chives and are an excellent choice for exposed northern gardens. Harvest leaves by cutting 1-2 inches above soil level, repeating every 3-4 weeks throughout the growing season; the flavor is essentially identical to common chives — mild onion — with the advantage that the broader leaves are easier to harvest in quantity. The large, globe-shaped flowerheads in late spring are highly attractive to native bees and can be used fresh as a garnish or steeped in vinegar; deadhead after flowering to maintain leaf production and prevent excessive self-seeding.

planting.note: Propagate from softwood or semi-hardwood cuttings taken in spring or summer; root cuttings in warm, moist medium and transplant to full sun, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed. In zones 8-9 plants may die back to the roots in frost but regrow vigorously from the base; in zones 10-11 trailing lantana is evergreen and blooms nearly year-round.

directions: Lantana montevidensis is a sprawling, trailing, woody-based perennial groundcover shrub native to South America, producing a near-continuous succession of dense, rounded clusters of small, lilac-purple flowers from late spring through hard frost — one of the longest and most reliable bloom periods of any ornamental shrub in warm climates, while also serving as an important nectar source for butterflies and hummingbirds. Propagate from cuttings in a warm, bright location; once transplanted to full sun and well-drained soil, trailing lantana is highly drought-tolerant and heat-loving, making it ideal for dry slopes, rock gardens, raised beds, and hanging baskets where it will cascade attractively over edges. Note that Lantana montevidensis is considered invasive in parts of the Gulf Coast (Florida, Texas, and Hawaii) where it escapes cultivation into natural areas — always check local regulations before planting outdoors, and prefer growing it in containers in regions where it may naturalize aggressively. Shear plants back by one-third in early spring to encourage fresh, vigorous growth and prevent woody, leggy stems; all parts of the plant are toxic if ingested, particularly the unripe green berries, so plant with caution in gardens accessible to children or pets.
